story:
In the sterile calm of a city morgue, Dr. Samuel Grayson found the order he craved. Stainless steel shone under soft lights, the air cool and faintly antiseptic, the hum of machines a steady lull. Here, the dead were puzzles with answers. The naked man on his table—no ID, no history, no visible wounds—should have been another case.

He began as always, voice steady into his recorder: male, thirty to forty, pale and almost translucent skin; faded tattoos—a star on the right shoulder, a sequence on the left forearm, “9468YA”; a healed abdominal scar suggesting prior surgery. He set his scalpel to the sternum for the Y-incision and pressed.

The blade skated over the skin like glass. No break. No give.

He tried a new blade, more pressure. Nothing. The flesh felt cold and wrong beneath his gloved fingers—unyielding, almost inorganic. When he pried open the eyelids, he reeled back. The eyes were totally black—no whites, no pupils—lightless pools that swallowed the room.

Science demanded persistence. He powered up the bone saw and touched it to the forehead.

Something in the air shifted. Pressure tightened around him; his ears popped. The lights flickered. He reached for his recorder to note the impossible resistance when the body jerked—one violent spasm, then it sat up, head turning toward him. A low, warping sound rattled from its throat, not a breath, not speech—like a voice trying to form in a frequency human mouths couldn’t hold.

Dr. Grayson froze.

Then the morgue melted.

Walls ran like paint. Cold steel became something wet and living. The overhead bulbs thinned into bioluminescent tendrils, throbbing a sickly green. The air stung with ammonia and unfamiliar chemicals. His head pounded under a slicing, internal sound. The new space snapped into focus.

He wasn’t standing over a body. He was strapped down to a platform.

The tools above him were no longer stainless steel but elongated, shifting things that seemed alive. He turned his head and saw them—tall, impossible silhouettes with too many joints and too many fingers, their eyes the same depthless black he had just seen.

A voice entered his mind without crossing air. Subject self-aware. Start sedation.

He tried to scream. Thick atmosphere swallowed the sound. Something pressed against his forehead, warmth rippling through bone. Memories shattered and flooded him at once—faces, places, words that didn’t fit, laughter that felt like grief. The morgue unraveled inside him as surely as it had around him.

He wasn’t in a morgue. He had never been in a morgue.

The corridor of calm, the stainless tables, the ritual of procedure—fabrications. A frame built to soothe a mind while it was examined. The tattoos, the scar, the uncuttable skin—markers and mechanisms, not mysteries. He was the subject, not the examiner. He was the one cataloged, tagged, and prepared.

Above him, the living tools drifted closer, their edges soft and hungry. The black-eyed figures watched, patient, unreadable. The thought landed, clear and cold.

This was an experiment.

And the body on the table had never been a dead man from an alley.

It had always been him.
